title: Young Gendo in Shin Eva
wikitext:
[]: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Mar 10, 2021 9:12 pm It feels like every month or so, we hear about a new reboot of some old franchise that provides a gritty backstory to a character. The internet collectively reacts in disgust, questioning why we need to know about the Joker's childhood or who asked for a gritty reboot of the Powerpuff girls. Origin stories for established characters that are interesting in part because we don't know their stories receive a great deal of negative attention (despite the money they make), and had any information about the scenes with young Gendo leaked, I have a feeling we might have experienced some of the same reactions. But oddly enough, when I was in the theater and I saw Shinji and Gendo begin to talk, I didn't feel like it was corny or cheesy or superfluous. I actually felt moved. In NGE, we do get some of Gendo's backstory during his time in university, but it's told to us through Fuyutsuki. We get to see a little bit of his personality-- wasn't he getting released by the police?-- and the tiniest bit of his interactions with Yui, but we never really get to see Gendo before any of this. We know when Yui is gone he's desperate to get her back, and not knowing his backstory plays into this well enough. He's just a man so desperate for his wife back that we can believe him as a sort of antagonist for the story while also having some degree of empathy for him. But NTE goes deeper, actually showing us what Gendo was like before he met Yui, and I actually think it makes him more sympathetic. There's something poignant about him listening to music, practicing the piano (!!), and not really caring about the world, and then having the pain of not being able to interact with it once Yui was gone that makes his motives more intense, I feel. I don't know if mapping NTE Gendo's experiences onto NGE Gendo makes sense, and something tells me it wouldn't, but even contained just within the new theatrical edition, there's something powerful about him talking about his own experiences. Even the scene where we actually see him set Shinji on the train feels welcome, rather than a retcon that nobody asked for. One last hug between Gendo and a young Shinji, sending him off with the SDAT player, was a strangely heartstring-tugging moment. I want to say there's some symbolism in how the door of the old Japanese train closes, how it shuts almost all the way, retracts slightly, and then sluggishly finishes closing before the train leaves the station-- maybe Gendo slightly regretting what he's done, then doggedly pressing forward? There might be an essay in there. The whole scene with the two of them talking before, during, and after these young Gendo flashbacks have plenty to unpack. How when Shinji begins, Gendo is on a modern train, but when he begins to talk more, the train is older, as it would have appeared during his youth. We all know the train is the inside of Shinji's consciousness, and it's interesting to see it shift when we're inside Gendo's. And then of course at the end of all this, when Shinji takes the SDAT and uses it to break through his father's own metaphysically manifested AT Field... there's something special about a son taking something he got from his father and found comfort in, and then using it to return some form of that same comfort to his father. Just Shinji actually talking to his father for once is cathartic in and of itself, but I enjoyed the answers we got as well. If you only have the subtitles, don't draw too many conclusions from what fan translators on the internet can produce. And even if you had professionally-made subtitles, you don't get any of the expressions on Gendo's face, the changing of the backgrounds, the musical score, all the things that make the scene so powerful. It even made me think about my relationship with my own dad (though we have a much healthier one, I promise.) I liked the scenes with young Gendo. It made me feel a deeper connection to the character and I feel my appreciation of these films has been enhanced, rather than degraded.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Wed Mar 10, 2021 10:21 pm I've heard some interesting stuff, I almost got the origin I wanted for him. Billy Butcher type but he wasn't a port baby like I wanted, but still good

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Kid » Wed Mar 10, 2021 11:32 pm I'm with you 100% Gah, I wish we could push Rebuild canon onto NGE, but I think it's just too humanizing to even take bits and pieces of Rebuild Gendo's past and apply it to NGE Gendo. NTE Gendo always seemed more introspective and more often than not he appears lost in thought, while NGE Gendo is more of an "eyes on the prize" type guy who cared solely about the mission. The direction they took is just soooo good.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by melonpan » Wed Mar 10, 2021 11:42 pm I didn't expect them to give Gendo his backstory outside of what they did with NGE, to be honest. I at least expected them to do NTE's version of Gendo's uni days but they went further with it and it seems great. I'm glad, especially since we hadn't seen much of him in NTE until now. I found that he was lacking some of the depth he had previously, even with the standards that NTE had with the lack of character development... Er, not really lack of development but rather, we don't know much about the characters this time since it isn't given to us, compared to NGE.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Pluto » Thu Mar 11, 2021 10:06 pm I'm really glad they went into the scene between the two. As you mentioned, it felt like it was something new and something that was supposed to happen that we haven't seen before. In the scenes you mentioned, it really imbues gendo's ambitions with something that's a lot more rooting in legitimate motivation and character than just I wanted to see Yui again. I remember coming back to an observation in EoE where it's implied that Gendo and Shinji are the same in terms of how the deal with the world and this time, we actually get to see how they are the same.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by JuanSorel » Thu Mar 11, 2021 11:49 pm Best scene until now with Gendo is episode 26. Yes, dear. Aha. Whatever you say dear. I am reading the newspaper, dear, leave me alone.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Zusuchan » Fri Mar 12, 2021 8:32 am Looks like the movie's gonna be quite interesting... Pluto wrote: it really imbues gendo's ambitions with something that's a lot more rooting in legitimate motivation and character than just I wanted to see Yui again. I remember coming back to an observation in EoE where it's implied that Gendo and Shinji are the same in terms of how the deal with the world and this time, we actually get to see how they are the same.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Pluto » Thu Mar 18, 2021 5:42 am It was really moving to see Gendo hug Shinji at the end of the train station at the end of his instrumentality sequence. It really puts a nice bow on his character arc. Moreover, it was also cool to see how much he looked like Shinji when he was younger.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by IsAnnoGodOrATroll » Thu Mar 18, 2021 8:58 am Pluto wrote: It was really moving to see Gendo hug Shinji at the end of the train station at the end of his instrumentality sequence. It really puts a nice bow on his character arc. Moreover, it was also cool to see how much he looked like Shinji when he was younger.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Pluto » Thu Mar 18, 2021 9:40 am IsAnnoGodOrATroll wrote: Pluto#916338 wrote: It was really moving to see Gendo hug Shinji at the end of the train station at the end of his instrumentality sequence. It really puts a nice bow on his character arc. Moreover, it was also cool to see how much he looked like Shinji when he was younger.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Tue Mar 23, 2021 7:54 am Pluto wrote: It was really moving to see Gendo hug Shinji at the end of the train station at the end of his instrumentality sequence. It really puts a nice bow on his character arc. Moreover, it was also cool to see how much he looked like Shinji when he was younger.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by one-eyed » Tue Mar 23, 2021 8:13 am You guys are really too romantic to fully forgive Gendo just because of a few scenes. I can't, I still find Gendo the most depraved, evil and disturbed character in all fiction.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Mar 23, 2021 8:27 am i dont think those scenes alone rly changed anyone's preexisting opinions that were already different from yours to begin with, they don't rly show anything that wasn't previously implied, they only elaborate. i highly doubt anyone imagined Gendo as having had an idyllic homelife & always being the life of the party. ...most depraved of ALL really? Including Ramsey Bolton? Griffith? All those Hitler knockoffs? No one's calling him a model citizen but "worst of all" seems like a failure of imagination.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by one-eyed » Tue Mar 23, 2021 9:26 am In my experience, who defends Gendo is passionate about the character and will defend him tooth and nail. I will not argue with you because I do not know the personal reasons that make you defend Gendo, but I do know that you will hate me if I say anything about him. The only thing I wish is that you will never find your own Gendo. May you never have such a man in your life because he will destroy everything and everyone around you: family, friends, job, everything. I’ve seen it happen in real life and it’s not pretty.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Tue Mar 23, 2021 9:33 am one-eyed wrote: You guys are really too romantic to fully forgive Gendo just because of a few scenes. I can't, I still find Gendo the most depraved, evil and disturbed character in all fiction.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Mar 23, 2021 9:47 am Sound's more like you're projecting / invested for personal reasons, not everyone else. Which is 'fair nuff, I guess. I wouldn't say that "there are worse villains" or "wow, this sequence was compelling" (a statement not on morality but on effective storytelling) is "defending" That's a pretty sober, neutral determination. I used to think pure evil was boring when I was younger; Then I lived through certain political events & realized that for all that it#s an abstraction (even if we're talking about things that come close like Nazis or exploitative giant corporations) the value of "pure evil" stories is in showing how everyone else deal or fails to deal with it - ie, great incompetence of the political establishment to deal with neo nazis... even the original nazis never had a majority, but the system was unstable & the other parties were too busy fighting each other to stop the threat, some of them though they could control the nazis for cheap gain & then the conservatives & monarchists were first against the wall... its like they tried to take the one ring almost. But the function of like relatable villains (not "sympathetic" or "justified" or even "grey", just... relatable) is different. You're sort of supposed to sympathize with those, recognize your own negative traits or the weaknesses/ confusions that might lead one down that path. & also to make you recognize the humanity even in your enemies lest you get judgemental. You can understand someone without implying that they're justified or ok. In fact, understanding why crimes are done how you decrease such things from happening in the future. I think being able to accept & epathize with the evil within yourself or what might lead someone to evil is crucial for self-awareness & avoiding self-delusion. Besides, they're playing sad music here, having Shinji say he really wants to understand, overcoming his fear that the answer is just banal I hate you - clearly they're expecting the audience to be sad. Anno's giving the big bad guy some of his own characteristics/flaws putting him in something looking like his old hometown; Clearly this was an exercise in this "confronting & showing empathy to your dark side" thing. I mean, there are those villains both in fiction and real life who really DO just hate you/ just not care. There's also ppl who fucked up because theyweren't given the resources to have good choices. Making one bad guy have a pitiful backstory doesn't mean that you gotta feel sorry for all bad guys now. And heck, even feeling sorry doesn't mean you have to go near them again, you can feel sorry & still stay tf away from someone No one's demanding you have to like him or can't dislike him anymore. You're the one who accused us all of being "romantic" and "forgiving" just cause ppl said they found the scene sad/compelling (different thing) which is rather insulting & arrogant, assuming you know all our inner workings & that we've never been hurt by any asshats

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by one-eyed » Tue Mar 23, 2021 11:05 am Kendrix wrote: Sound's more like you're projecting / invested for personal reasons, not everyone else. Which is 'fair nuff, I guess.

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by FXArmaros98 » Tue Mar 23, 2021 4:13 pm one-eyed wrote: Thank you for understanding. It took me a long time to admit to myself why I hated and despised Gendo so much, when the reason was obvious. I met someone that I can identify with him. It was an exposed nerve. Today, I avoid even thinking about the character if I can, but sometimes the words escape! P.S .: I saw Supernatural in the factorial style a little bit of each season and I think only up to 7 in full and up to 10 incomplete!

[]: Re: Young Gendo in Shin Eva             Quote Post by one-eyed » Tue Mar 23, 2021 4:48 pm True. The strange thing about Crowley is that he is the antagonist and is really evil, but you end up liking him more than Sam who is the deuteragonist!
